issues.description: Route handler has zero try/catch. Prisma errors (DB down, constraint violation) propagate as unhandled promise rejections. Fastify catches them and returns a raw 500, but structured error codes are lost. All four route files share this pattern — auth.ts, save.ts, leaderboard.ts, iap.ts each have 0 try/catch around DB calls.
VALID_PLATFORMS constant is copy-pasted verbatim in both auth.ts (line 10) and iap.ts (line 13). Should live in types/index.ts alongside the Platform type already defined there.

process.env.JWT_SECRET! uses a non-null assertion; if the env var is absent at startup the server will silently accept any secret or throw at the first JWT operation. No startup guard validates required env vars. DATABASE_URL falls back to a hardcoded dev string which is also undocumented. A config module or early startup check (throw if !JWT_SECRET) would harden this.

process.env.NODE_ENV and process.env.APPLE_IAP are read directly inside the route handler rather than through a config module. All other routes also read process.env inline (leaderboard, save read none; auth reads none; iap reads 2). No central config module exists.

LevelLoader.ts loads levels by dynamic-importing '../../../../.allforai/game-design/level-design.json' (136 levels, ids 1-1 through 6-23). The 180 generated level JSON files in game-client/assets/resources/levels/ch1–ch6/ are completely ignored. Levels 6-24 through 6-30 (and levels missing from the design JSON) will fall through to a 3-level inline fallback, causing '[LevelLoader] Level not found' errors at runtime for all generated content beyond the design file's range.

Two scheduleOnce() calls (lines 357, 370) queue callbacks that run after a delay. onDestroy() (line 179) does not call this.unscheduleAllCallbacks(). If the scene is destroyed before the timer fires (e.g. player exits mid-animation), the callbacks run on a destroyed Component and will throw or silently corrupt state.

scheduleOnce() used to stagger list-row fade-in animations (line 167, inside _renderList). onDestroy() (line 109) only removes button listeners; it does not call unscheduleAllCallbacks(). On rapid back-navigation, up to 50 pending callbacks can fire on a destroyed node.

File is 415 lines. It handles level loading, board initialization, obstacle wiring, event subscription, audio, combo tracking, session management, and result presentation in a single Component. Refactoring session-start and event-wiring into a GameSceneBootstrap helper would improve maintainability.

File is 587 lines and handles 8 distinct obstacle types (weed, wooden_crate, water_current, ice_block, chain_lock, portal, single_path_corridor, spreading_obstacle) plus water-current timer logic. Mixed responsibilities; consider splitting into per-obstacle-type handler classes or at least grouping by interface.

API_BASE_URL is hardcoded as a module-level constant in IAPManager.ts (line 11-13), ProgressionManager.ts (line 3-5), and LeaderboardScene.ts (line 22-24) — three separate copies of the same fallback URL. Should be a single shared constant (e.g. game-client/assets/scripts/util/ApiConfig.ts).

MAX_RETRIES (line 23), DIFFICULTY_FLOOR (line 28), and DIFFICULTY_CEILING (line 37) are all defined in pipeline.ts only, which is good (centralized in one file). However they are not exported, so any future test or diagnostic script cannot reference them without duplicating the values. Exporting them would cost nothing.

No test coverage exists for solver.ts or generator.ts. There are no .test.ts or .spec.ts files anywhere under tools/level-gen/. The beam-search solver (solver.ts) has a known approximation risk (it can miss solutions), making unit tests particularly valuable for regression detection.

CLI output is readable with icon/status/attempts/min_moves columns (formatRow, line 46-59). However when a level is rejected after MAX_RETRIES (25), the rejection_reason is logged via console.warn but not surfaced in the BatchResult row printed to stdout, making it hard to diagnose systemic generation failures without --verbose.

Field name mismatch in /iap/verify response: backend (iap.ts line 227) sends 'glowstone_granted', but IAPManager's VerifyResponse interface (line 52) declares 'beach_coins_granted'. The hourglass claim path also reads data.beach_coins_granted (line 201-202). The backend never sends 'beach_coins_granted', so the IAP grant logic in _verifyWithBackend will always receive 'undefined' for this field and silently apply 0 coins to the player balance after a real purchase.

IAPManager.claimHourglassReward() calls POST /v1/hourglass/claim (line 188) which does not exist in the backend. The backend has no hourglass route registered (verified by searching all backend/src/ files). In production mode (non-sandbox) this will throw SERVER_ERROR_404 and hourglass rewards will be completely unavailable.

ProgressionManager.loadFromCloud maps currency field 'glowstone' from backend response (line 179: data.currency.glowstone). Backend save GET response uses 'glowstone' (save.ts line 103: save.currency), but the SaveData type and client GameProgress use 'glowstones' (plural) internally. The mapping at line 179 is correct, but syncToCloud (line 311) sends 'glowstone' (singular) to match backend — this is consistent and correct. However, the local GameProgress.currency shape uses 'glowstones' while the initial_save field from auth.ts uses 'glowstone' (singular), and ProgressionManager.initAuth (line 89) reads data.initial_save.glowstone and assigns it to currency.glowstones — this mapping is correct but fragile with no type assertion.
